f   the two optic gnostic treatises   in   the   ruce ode,   the
ooks of eu and the ntitled et, only the latter has previously
been translated into nglish. he ruce ode has been known
to scholars since  in the ecellent edition and erman trans-
lation by . chmidt (ibl. ). he translation was re-edited by
. ill in  (ibl.   ·   ), but chmidt's edition of the tet has
been out of print for many years.   t   is therefore timely that there
has been a decision to republish the already known gnostic tets
in the ag ammadi tudies eries. chmidt's emended edition
of the tet has been reproduced here unaltered, and the present
nglish translation is based upon it.
or purposes of study and comparison with the erman,   the
general format of ibl.  has also been retained here. o facil-
itate reference to the erman version, all the indees are based
on the page numbers of chmidt's edition of the tet (ib!.   )
which are employed for both tet and translation in the present
volume. n ill's version (ibl. ) these page numbers appear
in the margin. ivision of the tet into paragraphs and numbered
chapters also follows ib!. .
lthough the present translation owes much to those of chmidt
and ill, and also to .   .   aynes (ib!. ), there are passages in
which some or all of the translations differ.   n   such cases   the
alternative readings or versions are given as footnotes. ill's notes
on chmidt's translation which appear in the ppendi to ib!. 
are also incorporated into the present footnotes. chmidt's foot-
notes to the optic tet are given   here   in   nglish   translation
his references to the readings by oide and chwartze who   first
 copied the tet have been omitted. aynes' version is of particular
value for her notes on the many obscure words and passages in
the ntitled et.
n the translation it will be seen that words of reek origin are
italicised. he alternative of giving the reek words in brackets
after the nglish words would have added considerably to   the
cost of publication.  key to these words of reek derivation   is
given on page . ertain reek words, some of which are gnostic
technical terms, have been left untranslated. ollowing chmidt

and ill, it was thought   preferable to give words of uncertain
meaning in transliteration, rather than to attempt a translation
which might be misleading.   t   has been possible in some cases
to indicate in the footnotes a comparable use of such words in
the ag ammadi tets and elsewhere.
n   addition,   in   the   ooks of eu   a special feature   are   the
numerous diagrams, together with untranslatable names, formulae
and vowel sequences. n chmidt's and ill's editions the reek
equivalents of the optic letters are given in the translation, and
the diagrams appear in both tet and translation. n a face   to
face edition, it seems unnecessary to reproduce in the translation
any material which can easily be read from the tet on the oppo-
site page.   or this reason,   in   the   present   version,   neither the
diagrams nor the untranslatable letter sequences have been given
twice. paces have been left on the pages of the translation which,
from their position, correspond to the diagrams in the adjacent
tet. imilarly, punctuated spaces  .   .   . in the translation indicate
omitted letter sequences. hese can be read from the tet in the
corresponding place on the page opposite where, for clarity, they
have been underlined.   t   should be noted that the diagrams   in
chmidt's edition are stylised versions of those in the manuscript.
hey differ considerably from the originals, both in configuration
and in their relation   to   the   tetual   matter   surrounding them.
hotography is the only satisfactory method of reproducing these
complicated figures, and it is hoped that a facsimile edition of the
tet will be possible.   ·,
 y acknowledgements are due to the urators of the odleian
ibrary, and to r. .   .   ay, enior ssistant ibrarian in the
epartment of riental ooks, for facilities to study the manu-
script. y thanks are due to r. .   .   dridge for his kind
interest during the preparation of this volume.  am grateful to
the ditorial oard of the ag ammadi tudies for the oppo-
tunity to contribute to their series, and especially to rofessor
. e. ilson who, as my volume editor, has given me most
generously of his time and helpful advice. ny errors remaining
in this volume are my responsibility.

istory of the ruce ode
 his code, which comprises optic, rabic and thiopic manu-
scripts, is said to have been bought at edinet abu in   pper
 gypt   in   about      by the   cottish   traveller,   ames   ruce. 
e owe this information to   .   . oide who made the first copy
of the optic gnostic tets contained in it. e also first brought
them to public notice with an article on the   gyptian   version
of the ible,  and he gave the biblical citations in his   ppendi
ad editionem ovi   estamenti.   fter his death his copy of the
tets was held by the larendon ress, ford, under the number
. larendon ress d.   . n  the code was acquired by
the   odleian   ibrary,   together   with   oide's   transcript   of the
gnostic tets. he gnostic manuscripts were catalogued under the
number ruce .
. . chwartze was the net to transcribe these tets when
he was in ngland in .   n   comparing oide's copy with the
originals, he found a number of mistakes, but his death unfor-
tunately occurred before his work was completed. is amended
copy became the property of .   .   etermann, at whose death
the copy finally came into the hands of   .   rman. 
n the meantime in  . melineau began to work on the
tet.   wo   preliminary   communications   appeared   in      and
, and in      an introduction to his translation of the tet
(ibl. ). he latter was published in  (ibl. ).
n      rman and arnack were instrumental   in arranging
that chmidt should work on the manuscript in   ford,   with
the support of the kademie der issenschaften of erlin. ith

the help of the copies made by oide and chwartze, chmidt
was able to distinguish that there were two manuscripts and some
fragments iri the one code. e put the leaves in sequence and
made a new transcript of the tets.   chmidt's page order was
later followed by the odleian authorities when, in   , they
renumbered the leaves. chmidt published his edition of the tet
with a erman translation and commentary in    (ibl. ).
o further editions of the whole tet have appeared.   n   
 chmidt published a revised translation (ibl.   ). he volume
contained translations of the istis ophia as well as the tets
of the ruce ode, together with commentaries on both codices.
wo   new   editions   by   ill   of this   volume   have   subsequently
appeared (see p. ).
n  an nglish translation of the manuscript known as the
ntitled et was published by . amplugh (ibl. ). his was
based on melineau's rench version.
 transcript and nglish translation of the ntitled et was
made by .   .   aynes in  (ibl. ).   he   based her work on
the original manuscript, using the published transcript by chmidt
and the copies of oide and   chwartze   for   comparison.   er
arrangement of the leaves differed from that of chmidt, in that
she placed his five final   leaves at the beginning. he ntitled
et was photographed at this   time,   and   photographic repro-
ductions of the leaves were included in this edition of the tet.
he other manuscript was photographed later.
 escription of the    anusc~fpt
 he   ruce ode originally consisted   of    papyrus   leaves
(    pages) of which seven leaves-in eistence when oide made
his copy-are now missing. ach page, with the eception of two,
is inscribed in one column, on both recto and verso. here are
from  to  lines to a page. oide noted that the condition of
the papyrus was poor, and in the course of a century the sub-
sequent deterioration of the manuscript, as recorded by chmidt,
aynes and ill, has been considerable.
hen acquired the code consisted of loose leaves, the original
order of which had been lost.   ne   leaf alone carried numbers,

and   oide   was   only   able   to   make a   page-by-page   transcript
without distinguishing the documents. n  the authorities of
the odleian ibrary caused the loose leaves of the code to be
bound in book form. he leaves were bound without regard to
order or sequence. any were placed upside-down and with the
recto and verso reversed.   ach leaf was enclosed between   two
sheets of tracing paper.
t is due to the work of   chmidt   that the code now stands
in its present form. he code consists of two independent manu-
scripts and some fragments. he first manuscript, to which   chmidt
 gave the title   "he   irst and   econd   ooks of   eu",   comprised
 leaves ( pages) of which   three   leaves   were   missing.   he
second, called the   "ntitled   et",   contained  leaves ( pages)
of which   four   were   missing.   chmidt   included   the   fragments
( leaves) with the first manuscript.
ach of the two main tets is written in a different hand, and
the two manuscripts bear no obvious relation   to one another.
he first is written in a cursive hand on papyrus of a pale colour.
he second is in an uncial script on a darker and more reddish
papyrus. ot only are the first and second manuscripts the work
of different scribes, but the fragments are in handwriting which
differs again from these. t the beginning of the first document
 chmidt   has placed a frontispiece consisting of a   leaf bearing
a cross in the form of an ankh sign. reek monograms occur
in relation to the arms of the cross.      he ooks of eu contain
a   number of cryptograms   and   gnostic diagrams.      leaf with
a border is placed by   chmidt   at the end of the ooks of   eu
 this contains the two fragments noted above.
he manuscript, re-bound and with its leaves renumbered,   is
now unfortunately in very poor condition. he papyrus of many
leaves is defective and there are opaque dark spots due to previous
mildew.   etails of the condition of individual leaves are given
in the footnotes on the optic tet. he writing is so faded as to
be almost illegible, even when viewed with ultra-violet light.
he title by which the first two tets are generally known does
not appear in the ruce ode.   t   is derived   from a reference

the title preserved this stands at the end and reads    "he   ook
of the great ogos corresponding to   ysteries".

.) and runs consecutively to   the   foot   of page      where   it

he tet of the econd ook of eu begins on page  (chmidt
.) and runs consecutively to the foot   of page    (chmidt
.). he end of the tet is missing.   n   the single leaf which
follows, page  contains a fragment of a gnostic hymn (chmidt
.-.), and on page  is a description of the passage of
the soul (chmidt .-.).
he ntitled et lacks both beginning and end.   ccording
to the pagination of chmidt, the tet runs consecutively from
pages  to  (chmidt .-.). ages - are five leaves
of uncertain relation to the rest of the tet, which chmidt places
at   the   end   (chmidt   .-.).   n   her   edition   of the   tet
(ibl. ) aynes places these leaves at the beginning, but for the
reasons given below the page order of chmidt and ill has been
retained here.

oth tets of the ruce ode appear to be compilations, and
similar or related   "documents"   are either grouped together or
placed one following another in sequence. ccasionally the present
chapter divisions may indicate   where   one   document   ends   and
another begins. his method of composition gives rise to repetitions
in the narrative, and to a lack of overall continuity. hus in the
ooks of eu, hapters - appear to be variant accounts of
what has already been given in hapters f.   n the   ntitled
et, hapters  and      may be different descriptions of the same
phenomena. s the unplaced leaves forming hapter  contain
material similar to that in hapters   ,   , , and   ,   and   are
perhaps part of a separate version of the whole tet,   it seems
appropriate to place them at the end of the treatise.
 brief summary of the contents of the ooks of eu and the
ntitled et   is given here for   the convenience of the reader.
n attempt has been made to indicate the most outstanding motifs
in each chapter, but in many cases the selection   is necessarily
a rather arbitrary one.

to   his   disciples   they   were   very   sorrowful,   and   they   prostrated
themselves at the feet of esus, crying out and weeping. hey said 
 "   ord, why hast thou not said to us   will give to you the
 mysteries   of the   reasury   of the   ight"
 ut   the heart of esus was   sorrowful   over his   disciples,   because
they had left behind them their fathers and their brothers, and
their wives and their children, and   they   had   left behind   them
the whole   life   of this   world*   and   they   had   followed   him   for
twelve years, and they had fulfilled all the   injunctions   with which
he had enjoined them.
e answered and said to his   disciples   "ruly    say to you
 will give to you the   mysteries   of the nine   watchers   of the three
 gates   of the   reasury   of the ight, and the manner (of calling
upon them in order to go to their places). nd also  will give
to you the   mysteries   of the child of the child    ,   and the manner
(of calling   upon   them   in   order   to   go   to   their   places).   nd
furthermore after these things  will give to you the   mystery   of
the three   amens,   and the manner (of calling upon them in order
to go to their places). nd also  will give to you the   mystery
 of the five (trees) of the   reasury   of the ight, and the manner
(of calling   upon   them   in   order   to   go   to   their   places).   nd
furthermore after these things  will give to you the   mystery   of
the seven   voices   and the   will   of the    powers     •   nd also  will
give to you the   mystery   of the great name of all names, which   is
the great light which surrounds the   reasury   of the ight, and
the manner (of calling upon it) in order to go to the interior of
the seven   voices.
 nd   truly    say to you and  command you that you perform
the   mystery   of the five trees, and the   mystery   of the seven   voices.
 and the   mystery   of the great name which is the great light which
surrounds the   reasury   of the ight.   or   he who will   perform
these will not   need   any other   mysteries   of the ingdom of the
ight,   ecept for   the   mystery   of the forgiveness of sins  .

or   it   is   necessary   that   every   man   who   will   believe   in   the
ingdom of the ight should perform the   mystery   of the forgive-
ness of sins only once.   or   to every man who will perform the
 mystery   of the forgiveness of sins, all the sins which he has com-
mitted   knowingly   or   unknowingly ,   from   his   childhood   until
 today, and which he has committed since the   foundation   of the
 world until   today, will all be erased, and he will be made to be
a   pure   light and taken to the ight of these ights. nd  say
to you that since they were on earth they have already   inherited
 the ingdom of od. hey have their   part   in the   reasury   of
the ight, and they are   immortal   gods. nd when those who have
received these   mysteries   and the   mystery   of the forgiveness of sins
come forth from the   body,   all the   aeons   draw back (one) after
another, and they flee to the west to the left  on account of the
 soul   which has received the   mystery   of the forgiveness of sins,
until they (the souls) reach the   gates   of the   reasury   of the ight,
and the   watchers   of the   gates   open to them.
hen they reach the   ranks   of the   reasury,   the   ranks   also   seal
 them with their   seal   and give to them the great name of their
 mystery,   and they pass into their interior.
hen they reach the   rank   of the five trees of the   reasury   of
the ight, they give to them the great name and they   seal   them
with their   seal,   and they give to them   their   mystery   until   they
pass into the interior of the seven   voices.
 hen they reach that   rank,   they give to them the great name.
nd they   seal   them with their   seal   and they give to them their
 mystery   until   they   pass   into   the   interior of the   ranks   of the
 fatherless ones, as far as   the   rank   of the   places   of their   inheritance.

 invisible one,   from which all   invisible ones   have come.

 monads   within it (the pleroma), one   monad   to each   gate   and si
 helpers (parastatai)     to each   gate,   making    helpers (parastatai)

 worlds   which surround them from the light of the   only-begotten
one      hidden within him, this one whom they seek after.

pleroma.   nd they saw the light of the   light-spark.   hey rejoiced

forms     and   pure one,   and they called him   unmoved one,   and all

 light-spark   out of the   pleroma.   nd the   triple-powered one   came 
down to the   places   of the   self-begotten one.   nd they saw the   grace
 of the   aeons   of the light which was   granted   to them. hey rejoiced
because he who eists came forth among them.
 hen   the   veils   opened, and the light penetrated down   to   the
 matter   below and to those who had no form   and no likeness.
nd in this way they acquired the likeness of the   light.   ome
 indeed   rejoiced because the light came to them and they became
rich.   thers   wept because they became poor, and   those things
which they had were taken away. nd this is the way it happened
to the   grace   which   came   forth.   herefore   captivity   was   taken

trempsuchos     ,   gramas    and   those   with   him.   hey   became
 helpers     to those who   believed   in the   light-spark.

 mind   is revealed  in it.

the   land   on the right side of him, and he   granted   to them eternal

nd he called the   land   on the right side   "the   land   of   light,"   and
that on the left   "the   land   of   darkness."     nd he called the   land
 on the right side   "the   land   of   rest,"   and the   land   on   the   left
 "the   land   of   toil."   nd he set boundaries between them, and   veils
 between them, so that they should not see one another. nd he
placed   watchers   upon   "their   veils.   nd   he   gave   many   honours
to those who had worshipped him.   nd he ealted   them over
those who had   opposed   him and withstood him. nd he spread
out the   land   on the right side into many   lands.   nd he made them
